---
title: "BLISS-RS Formulation"
---

```{julia}
#| include: false
include("src/bliss_rs.jl")
include("src/supersonic_jet.jl")
using Printf, Plots, LaTeXStrings
gr()
```

## Motivation: From Gradients to Surrogates

BLISS-98 couples the local and system levels through sensitivity derivatives
$\partial X^*_{opt} / \partial Z$. Computing these derivatives requires:

1. A converged system analysis (expensive).
2. A local optimization at each BBs (moderately expensive).
3. A sensitivity analysis — typically finite differences or adjoint solves — at the
   local optimum (often the most expensive step).

These three requirements limit the practical applicability of BLISS-98 in two ways.
First, the sensitivity analysis must be repeated each BLISS cycle, making the method
computationally intensive for BBs with many variables. Second, because the coupling
relies on a *local* linear approximation, the algorithm can be sensitive to the
accuracy of the linearization and may stall near saddle points.

BLISS-RS addresses both limitations by replacing the gradient-based coupling with
**quadratic response surface surrogates** [@altus2002blissrs]. The key observations
motivating this change are:

- A response surface efficiently *explores the entire design space region* by
  evaluating the BB at many sample points rather than linearizing around a single
  point.
- Because BBs in BLISS-RS are optimized off-line for each DOE point independently,
  those optimizations can proceed in strict parallel — the algorithm becomes a
  *workfront* that saturates available computing resources.
- The RS coupling eliminates the need for a system sensitivity analysis. The system
  optimizer reads coupling information directly from the polynomial coefficients.

## Local Optimization Formulation

In BLISS-RS, each black box $k$ defines a *synthetic objective function* that
aggregates all of its outputs into a single scalar using weighting factors $w$. For
given fixed values of $Z$, $Y^*$, and $w$, the $k$-th BB solves:

$$
\text{Given:} \quad Z, \; Y^*, \; w
$$ {#eq-local-given}

$$
\text{Find:} \quad X \;\text{(local to BB}_k\text{)}
$$ {#eq-local-find}

$$
\text{Minimize:} \quad F_k = \sum_i w_i \, Y^A_i
$$ {#eq-local-obj}

$$
\text{Satisfy:} \quad \{h\} = 0, \quad \{g\} \leq 0, \quad X_L \leq X \leq X_U
$$ {#eq-local-constraints}

The term *synthetic* is used because the objective $F_k$ does not represent the
actual system objective (range, for example). Instead, it is a proxy that drives
each BB toward outputs that are beneficial to the system. The weighting factors $w$
encode the relative importance of each BB output to the overall system objective.

::: {.callout-note}
**Weighting factor interpretation.** In the supersonic business jet context, the
structures BB outputs total weight $W_T$ and aeroelastic twist $\Omega$. Both affect
range through the Breguet equation. The weight assigned to each ($w_1$ for $W_T$,
$w_2$ for $\Omega$) indicates how strongly minimizing that output will benefit the
system objective. The system optimizer determines the appropriate $w$ values
automatically as part of its decision variables.
:::

The synthetic objective is evaluated at many points $(Z, Y^*, w)$ by running the
local optimization for each DOE sample. The collection of optimal values
$F_k^*(Z, Y^*, w)$ forms the data cloud from which the RS is fitted.

## System Optimization Formulation

After the RS models are available, the system optimizer solves:

$$
\text{Given:} \quad \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_k \;\text{for each BB } k
$$ {#eq-sys-given}

$$
\text{Find:} \quad Z, \; Y^*, \; w
$$ {#eq-sys-find}

$$
\text{Maximize:} \quad F = \text{Range}(Z, Y^*, w) \;\text{via RS models}
$$ {#eq-sys-obj}

$$
\text{Satisfy:} \quad \{c\} = Y^A - Y^* = 0, \quad \{g\} \leq 0, \quad \text{side constraints}
$$ {#eq-sys-constraints}

where $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_k$ are the regression coefficient vectors for the
$k$-th BB's response surface model. The coupling constraints $\{c\} = 0$ ensure that
the value of each coupling variable proposed by the system optimizer ($Y^*$) matches
the value that the corresponding BB would actually produce ($Y^A$) given those inputs.
In @fig-sys-optimizer, coupling constraints are represented as circles where data
flow channels from different BBs meet.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-sys-optimizer
%%| fig-cap: "BLISS-RS system optimizer data flow. The system optimizer proposes shared variables Z and coupling targets Y*, which are read from the quadratic RS models. Coupling constraints (○) enforce Y* = Y^A at convergence. Reproduced from Altus (2002) Fig. 11."
%%| fig-width: 6.5

flowchart TB
    SysOpt(["**System Optimizer**\nProposes Z · Y* · w"])

    subgraph rsmods ["  Response Surface Models  "]
        direction LR
        S["**Structures RS**\nOutputs: W_T, Ω, W_F"]
        A["**Aero RS**\nOutputs: L, D, L/D"]
        P["**Power RS**\nOutputs: SFC, W_E, ESF"]
    end

    Perf[/"**Performance**\n(Breguet Range)"/]

    SysOpt -- "Z" --> S & A & P

    S -- "W_T, Ω" --> A
    P -- "W_E" --> S
    A -- "D" --> P
    P -- "ESF" --> A

    S -- "W_F" --> Perf
    A -- "L/D" --> Perf
    P -- "SFC" --> Perf

    Perf -- "Range" --> SysOpt

    classDef sysopt fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#2e1065
    classDef rsmod  fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1e3a5f
    classDef aero   fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#14532d
    classDef power  fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef perf   fill:#fff1f2,stroke:#e11d48,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#4c0519

    class SysOpt sysopt
    class S rsmod
    class A aero
    class P power
    class Perf perf
```

## Weighting Factors as System Degrees of Freedom

One of the most important conceptual shifts in BLISS-RS relative to BLISS-98 is
the treatment of weighting factors $w$. In BLISS-98, the weighting factors that
define each BB's composite objective function are held fixed — they are determined
a priori by the analyst based on engineering judgement about the relative importance
of each output.

In BLISS-RS, the weighting factors $w$ are *system-level decision variables*,
optimized alongside $Z$ and $Y^*$ by the system optimizer. This means the system
optimizer can autonomously determine how much each BB should favour, for example,
minimizing weight versus maximizing lift-to-drag.

This autonomy comes at a cost: the design space for the system optimizer grows to
include $|Z| + |Y^*| + \sum_k |w_k|$ variables, which increases the complexity of
the RS fitting. However, because the system RS is evaluated analytically (not
through expensive BB calls), this additional complexity is acceptable.

## BLISS-RS Flowchart

@fig-blissrs-flow summarizes the complete algorithm. The critical path of the
computation is the BB optimization loop; all other operations are
inexpensive polynomial evaluations.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-blissrs-flow
%%| fig-cap: "BLISS-RS algorithm flowchart. Local BB optimizations (green) run concurrently at every DOE sample point. The system optimizer reads the fitted RS models — no sensitivity derivatives are required. Reproduced from Altus (2002) Fig. 10."
%%| fig-width: 4.5

flowchart TD
    A([Initialise X, Z, Y, w\nSet LB₀, UB₀])
    B["Optional: System Analysis\n(initialise coupling Y)"]
    C["Set bounds on Y\nGenerate D-Optimal DOE"]
    E["Fit quadratic RS\nto each BB output"]
    F["System Optimisation\nMaximise objective over RS"]
    G{"Converged?\n|Y_RS − Y_BB| ≤ ε"}
    H["Final BB Analysis\nRetrieve local X"]

    A --> B --> C
    C --> BB1 & BB2 & BBn
    BB1 & BB2 & BBn --> E
    E --> F --> G
    G -- Yes --> H --> I([Stop])
    G -- "No — reduce\nintervals" --> C

    subgraph con ["  Concurrent BB evaluations  "]
        BB1["BB₁  Local\nOptimisation"]
        BB2["BB₂  Local\nOptimisation"]
        BBn["BBₙ  Local\nOptimisation"]
    end

    classDef init fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1e3a5f
    classDef proc fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef bb   fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#14532d
    classDef fit  fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1e3a5f
    classDef done fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d
    classDef dec  fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006

    class A init
    class B,C,F proc
    class BB1,BB2,BBn bb
    class E fit
    class H,I done
    class G dec
```

## Comparison: BLISS-98 vs. BLISS-RS

| Feature | BLISS-98 | BLISS-RS |
|---------|----------|---------|
| BB-to-system coupling | Sensitivity derivatives | Quadratic RS surrogates |
| System analysis required per cycle | Yes | No (RS serves this role) |
| Local optimizations per cycle | 1 per BB | $N_S$ per BB (parallelisable) |
| Gradient computation required | Yes | No |
| Handles non-differentiable BBs | No | Yes |
| Prone to local optima | More so | Less so |
| Weighting factors $w$ | Fixed by analyst | Optimised by system level |
| Primary computational cost | Sensitivity analysis | RS generation |

: Comparison of BLISS-98 and BLISS-RS key features {#tbl-comparison}

The central tradeoff is that BLISS-RS requires many more BB evaluations per cycle
(one per DOE point per BB) compared to BLISS-98 (one per BB). However, because
those evaluations are fully concurrent and because the sensitivity analysis step is
eliminated, BLISS-RS is expected to achieve better wall-clock performance on
multi-processor systems.
